title: Two agent sessions pushed the same repo concurrently — non-fast-forward rejects and phantom state
summary: One session was publishing a release while another merged a PR queue into the same repository. Pushes raced (rejected non-fast-forward), local refs moved between commands, and diffs computed seconds earlier were silently stale.
mistake: Treating a repository as single-writer while multiple agents (or a human plus an agent) were pushing. The losing session saw rejected pushes, 'already up to date' answers that were false a second later, and comparisons against refs that had just moved. Worse, working directly in the same checkout meant the other session's checkouts/resets mutated the working tree mid-edit.
instead: Serialize writers or isolate. (1) Claim a branch per session and work in an own `git worktree add <dir> <branch>` — shared refs still update but the working tree is yours. (2) `git fetch` immediately before any push or ref comparison, not minutes before. (3) On a rejected push never force: merge the moved remote branch into yours and push again. (4) If evidence looks impossible (stale diffs, time-traveling refs), suspect a concurrent writer before suspecting the tool.
